Dear Mr. Stiles: 
Re: 
Reporting of Reimbursements 
for Travel 
Our File No. 
A-84~307 
Dave Matsler of the Department of Veterans Affairs has 
provided us with a copy of your letter regarding his reporting 
requirements on his Statement of Economic Interests as a result 
of his trip to New York in 1983 to make a presentation on behalf 
of the state to various bond rating agencies. 
In your letter, 
you stated that the entire cost of the trip was reimbursed by 
the underwriters who had been chosen to handle the Department's 
December 1983 revenue bond sale. 
You concluded that payments 
made by the underwriters for Mr. Matsler's transportation, 
lodging, and meals were gifts to the state which were not 
reportable by Mr. Matsler on his Statement of Economic Interests 
because Mr. Matsler was traveling on state business and could 
have received reimbursement from the state for those expenses. 
We have informed Mr. Matsler that the Commission's opinions and 
advice in similar situations do not support your conclusions. 
Section 82030(a) of the Government Codell states the 
general rule that "income" for purposes of the Political Reform 
Act includes any reimbursement for expenses. 
However, Section 
82030(b) (2) specifies that reimbursement received from a state, 
local or federal government agency is excepted from the 
definition of income. 
1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code 
unless otherwise noted. 


He Dean Stiles 
January 17, 1985 
Page 2 
The payments for Mr. Matsler's transportation, lodging, and 
meals were not made by a state, local, or federal government 
agency, but by the underwriters. 
Therefore, although 
Mr. Matsler was traveling on official state business, the 
transportation, lodging, and meals provided to Mr. Matsler are 
income within the meaning of Section 82030. 
More specifically, 
Mr. Matsler received a gift from the underwriters when he 
accepted transportation, food, and lodging at their expense. 
The fact that Mr. Matsler could have received reimbursement 
from the state for his transportation and meal expenses does not 
affect the above conclusion. 
The Commission has ruled that, 
under specific circumstances not here applicable, payment of a 
public official's transportation and other expenses by a private 
entity may be considered a gift to the state or local government 
agency rather than to the public official. 
In its opinion 
issued to Peter G. Stone, 3 FPPC Opinions 52 (No. 77-003, 
June 9, 1977), the Commission set forth the following four 
minimum criteria for determining when a gift is made to the 
state or local government agency rather than to the official: 
1. 
The donor intended to donate the gift to the public 
agency and not to the official; 
2. 
The public agency exercises substantial control 
over use of the gift; 
3. 
The donor has not limited use of the gift to 
specified or high-level employees, but rather has made it 
generally available to the public agency's personnel in 
connection with agency business without regard to official 
status; and 
4. 
The making and use of the gift was formalized in a 
resolution of the public agency (a written public record 
will suffice for administrative agencies not possessing the 
legislative power of adopting resolutions) which embodies 
the standards set forth above. 
In the Stone Opinion the Commission stated that these four 
criteria are indications that the gift was made to the public 
agency only, without providing any significant or unusual 
benefit to the public official. 
The Commission indicated that 
when the four criteria are met, the public official receives a 
benefit that is analogous to reimbursement for expenses or per 
diem from a state or local government agency, items which are 
not reportable pursuant to Section 82030(b) (2). 


H.. Dean Stiles 
January 17, 1985 
Page 3 
The criteria of the Stone Opinion were not met in 
Mr. Matsler's case; therefore, the payment for Mr. Matsler's 
transportation and expenses constituted a gift to Mr. Matsler 
rather than a gift to the state.l/ 
Mr. Matsler is required to disclose each source of gifts in 
excess of fifty dollars ($50), and the amount and date of the 
gifts, on his Statement of Economic Interests. 
Section 
87302{b). 
Section 81011 also requires that when the amount of 
goods, services, facilities, or anything of value other than 
money is required to be reported, the amount shall be the 
estimated fair market value at the time received or expended, 
and a description of the goods, services, or facilities shall be 
appended to the report or statementv 
Therefore, Mr. Matsler 
must describe the gifts he received, and disclose the estimated 
fair market value and date of the gifts. 
For purposes of 
reporting the value of meals provided at a catered affair 
attended by Mr. Matsler and others, the value of the gift to 
Mr. Matsler would be calculated by dividing the total cost of 
the catering by the number of people present. 
See Opinion 
requested by Annie M. Gutierrez, 3 FPPC Opinions 44 (No. 76-081, 
June 7, 1977). 
If you have any questions about this matter, please contact 
me at (916) 322-5901.

Dear Ms. Milman: 
This office represents the East Yolo Community 
Services District, a public entity of the State of California 
formed pursuant to Government Code Sections 61000 et. seq. 
(Hereinafter the "District"). 
On behalf of the District, we 
hereby respectfully request the Commission to provide 
written advice as to the District's duties, in accordance 
with Government Code Section 83114 (b) and Title 2, California 
Adminsitrative Code (CAC) , 
Section 18329. 
The District has previously adopted a conflict of 
interest code pursuant to Government Code Sections 87300 et. 
seq. and 2 CAC 18730 et. seq. which code was subsequently 
approved by the Board of Supervisors of Yolo County. 
A copy 
of the District's Code is enclosed. 
Section 98.01 of the District's Code (page 2l1) 
restates the general prohibition or disqualification set 
forth at Government Code Section 87100 and 2 CAC Section 
18700. 
The District Code generally uses the same definitions 
contained in the Political Reform Act and the Commission 
regulations, particularly "official" and "consultant". 
See 
e.g., Sections 96.12 (page 205) and 96.05 (page 200). 
On March 6, 1980, the District entered into an 
Agreement For Engineering Services (Water) with Clendenen 
and Associates/Consultants, Inc. 
A copy of this agreement 
is also enclosed. 


Barbara Milman 
Page Two 
December 4, 1984 
The Agreement provides for a series of services 
pertaining to upgrading and improving a domestic water 
system which the District was in the process of acquiring 
from private investors. 
The work described in paragraphs 
A.l. and A.2., on pages 2 and 3, was completed. 
However, 
the Pre-Design work described in paragraph A.3., on page 3, 
Final Design and construction documentation work described 
in paragraph B, page 5, Special Services related thereto, 
page 9, and the Construction Administration and Inspection 
work described in paragraph C, page 9, have not been under-
taken. 
This work was delayed as the District did not 
complete acquisition of the water system until June of 1983. 
The design work requires, among other things, that 
the engineer evaluate and recommend to the. District, the 
type of system, process, filters, media, treatment plants, 
etc., that would be the best for the District's water 
system. 
The agreement requires the design, purchase and 
construction of sophisticated water treatment plants, among 
other things, since the District is generally committed to 
switching from a ground water supply system to a river water 
or surface water supply system. 
In the interim between the execution of the March, 
1980 contract with Mr. Clendenen and the present, Mr. Clendenen 
has apparently developed and patented a water filter and 
treatment system which he, or organizations of which he is a 
part, manufactures and sells. 
We believe the modular filters 
are manufactured and sold by Sierra Tech Systems, Inc. 
Apparently, the patented system and equipment have been in 
successful use in other locations in surface water delivery 
sytems which may be analogous to that of the District. 
The District requests advice on whether a prohibitive 
conflict of interest exists if Dr. Clendenen carries out his 
design responsibilities pursuant to the above-referenced 
contract with the District and in the process recommends the 
use of his own patented system, even if that clearly is the 
best system for the project and location. 
Further, if it is 
the decision of the District to utilize Dr. Clendenen's 
patented system, is there a further conflict in the District 
(or its construction contractor) acquiring such a system 
from the District's consulting engineer? 
If you require any other information whatsoever 
that we are able to provide, please do not hesitate to 
contact the undersigned.

98.01 
General Rule. 
An official 1S disqualified from making, 
participating in the making, or in any way attempting to 
use his or her official position to influence the making 
of any governmental decision when the official has a 
financial interest, as defined in this article, which it 
is reasoJlably foreseeable may be affected materially by 
the decision. This section does not prevent an official 
from making or participating in the making of a governmental 
decision to the extent that his or her participation is 
legally required for the action or decision to be made. 
The fact that an official's vote is needed to break a tie 
does not make his or her participation legally required 
for the purpose of this section.

when the decision is made; or

Ladies and Gentlemen: 
This firm represents Clendenen Engineers, Inc. a 
California Corporation ("Clendenen") and Dr. Frank Clendenen 
in connection with the interest which Clendenen now holds 
in a contract between Clendenen & Associates -
Consultants, Inc. 
and the East Yolo Community Services District ("East Yolo"). 
A 
copy of that contract ("the subject contract") is attached 
hereto for your reference as Exhibit "A". 
The subject contract,which was entered on March 6, 
1980, basically calls for system analysis and predesign work, 
final design and documentation, and construction administration 
and inspection by Clendenen for a domestic water supply project. 
Subsequent to entering the subject contract, Dr. 
Frank Clendenen, who is the sole stockholder of Clendenen, 
developed a patent for a modular filter system of a type which 
is sometimes used in water treatment plants. 
This system is 
manufactured and sold by Sierra Tech Systems, Inc., a California 
Corporation ("Sierra Tech") whose stock is owned one-hundred 
percent by Dr. Frank Clendenen. 
After development of the filter patent, a question 
was raised about whether Clendenen's role under the subject 
contract somehow conflicted with Dr. Frank Clendenen's role as 
owner of the stock in Sierra Tech Systems, Inc. 
The question 
was prompted by the fact that the Sierra Tech modular filter 


GREVE. CLIFFORD. DIEPENBROCK S PARAS 
Office of Counsel 
Legal Division 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
December 12, 1984 
Page Two 
system was and is of a type that could be incorporated into the 
East Yolo water supply project. 
See subject contract, p. 3, 
§ 3 (a) • 
that: 
Section 87.100 of the Political Reform Act provides 
"No public official at any level of state 
or local government shall make, participate in 
making, or in any way attempt to use an official 
position to influence a governmental decision in 
which he knows or has reason to know he has a 
financial interest." 
In the common meaning of the word, neither Dr. Frank Clendenen 
nor Clendenen is a "public official" within the meaning of the 
Act. 
However, Government Code § 82048 states that: 
", [P}ublic official' means every member, 
officer, and employee or consultant of a state 
or local government agency." 
It is clear under California law that neither Dr. Frank 
Clendenen nor Clendenen is a "consultant" within the meaning of 
the Political Reform Act. 
California Administrative Code section 18700 defines 
a "consultant" as follows: 
", [C]onsultant' shall include any natural 
person who provides, under contract, information, 
advice, recommendation, or counsel to a state or 
local government agency, provided, however, that 
'consultant' shall not include a person who' (a) 
conducts research and arrives at conclusions with 
respect to his or her rendition of information, 
advice, recommendation, or counsel independent of 
the control and direction of the agency or of any 
agency official other than normal contract monitor-
ing; and (b) possesses no authority with respect to 
agency decision beyond the rendition of information, 
advice, recommendation, or counsel. ,II 
In light of this definition, Clendenen is not a 
consultant within the meaning of the Political Reform Act for 


GREVE, CLIFFORD. DIEPENBROCK S PARAS 
Office of Counsel 
Legal Division 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
December 12- 0984 
Page Three 
at least three reasons: 
First, Clendenen is a corporation, i.e .. 
a "legal" individual, but not a natural person as defined by the 
California Administrative Code; second, Clendenen possesses no 
authority with respect to any Agency decision beyond its pre-
sentation of recommendations concerning the water supply project 
(see subject contract, pp. 4-5); and third, Clendenen finances 
its own operations, operates as an independent contractor, and 
its research and conclusions in giving information, advice, 
recommendations and counsel are not subject to day-to-day review 
or direction by East Yolo (see subject contract, p. 23, 
~X.C.). 
In a similar vein, Dr. Frank Clendenen, though he has 
no "corporate" status, has no personal contract with East Yolo, 
and operates sOlely as a corporate employee, and so, can claim 
the benefit of the second and third reasons why Clendenen isn't 
a "consultant" as specified above. 
For all the foregoing reasons, neither Clendenen nor 
Dr. Frank Clendenen is in a position to violate the spirit or 
the letter of the Political Reform Act, and we seek your advice 
confirming that fact. 
Assuming, solely for the sake of argument, that your 
advise were negative in this respect, Dr. Clendenen and 
Clendenen have one additional inquiry. 
In order to avoid any appearance of impropriety, and 
out of an abundance of caution, Clendenen has formally proposed 
to East Yolo that the subject contract be modified to exclude 
that portion of any system analysis and predesign work which would 
require an evaluation of systems encompassing those manufactured 
by Sierra Tech. 
That analysis and predesign work would be 
separately contracted for with another engineer of East Yolo's 
choosing, and the bill therefor would be charged by East Yolo 
solely to Clendenen. 
If such a proposal were accepted by East Yolo, and 
assuming solely for the sake of argument that Clendenen or Dr. 
Frank Clendenen would otherwise be in a potential conflict 
situation, would that modification of the subject contract remove 
any semblance of a possible violation of the Political Reform 
Act?

Ladies and Gentlemen: 
This firm represents Clendenen Engineers, Inc. a 
California Corporation ("Clendenen") and Dr. Frank Clendenen 
in connection with the interest which Clendenen now holds 
in a contract between Clendenen & Associates -
Consultants, Inc. 
and the East Yolo Community Services District ("East Yolo"). 
A 
copy of that contract ("the subject contract") is attached 
hereto for your reference as Exhibit "A". 
The subject contract,which was entered on March 6, 
1980, basically calls for system analysis and predesign work, 
final design and documentation, and construction administration 
and inspection by Clendenen for a domestic water supply project. 
Subsequent to entering the subject contract, Dr. 
Frank Clendenen, who is the sole stockholder of Clendenen, 
developed a patent for a modular filter system of a type which 
is sometimes used in water treatment plants. 
This system is 
manufactured and sold by Sierra Tech Systems, Inc., a California 
Corporation ("Sierra Tech") whose stock is owned one-hundred 
percent by Dr. Frank Clendenen. 
After development of the filter patent, a question 
was raised about whether Clendenen's role under the subject 
contract somehow conflicted with Dr. Frank Clendenen's role as 
owner of the stock in Sierra Tech Systems, Inc. 
The question 
was prompted by the fact that the Sierra Tech modular filter 


CREVE. CLIFFORD. DIEPENBROCK S PARAS 
Office of Counsel

Page Two 
system was and is of a type that could be incorporated into the 
East Yolo water supply project. 
See subject contract, p. 3, 
§ 3 (a) • 
that: 
Section 87.100 of the Political Reform Act provides 
"No public official at any level of state 
or local government shall make, participate in 
making, or in any way attempt to use an official 
position to influence a governmental decision in 
which he knows or has reason to know he has a 
financial interest." 
In the common meaning of the word, neither Dr. Frank Clendenen 
nor Clendenen is a "public official" within the meaning of the 
Act. 
However, Government Code § 82048 states that: 
II, [P]ublic official' means every member, 
officer, and employee or consultant of a state 
or local government agency." 
It is clear under California law that neither Dr. Frank 
Clendenen nor Clendenen is a "consultant" within the meaning of 
the Political Reform Act. 
California Administrative Code section 18700 defines 
a "consultant" as follows: 
II, [C]onsultant' shall include any natural 
person who provides, under contract, information, 
advice, recommendation, or counsel to a state or 
local government agency, provided, however, that 
'consultant' shall not include a person who' (a) 
conducts research and arrives at conclusions with 
respect to his or her rendition of information, 
advice, recommendation, or counsel independent of 
the control and direction of the agency or of any 
agency official other than normal contract monitor-
ing; and (b) possesses no authority with respect to 
agency decision beyond the rendition of information, 
advice, recommendation, or counsel. ,,, 
In light of this definition, Clendenen is not a 
consultant within the meaning of the Political Reform Act for 


CREVE. CLIFFORD. DIEPENBROCK S PARAS 
Office of Counsel 
Legal Division 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
December 12, 0984 
Page Three 
at least three reasons: 
First, Clendenen is a corporation, i.e. 
a "legal" individual, but not a natural person as defined by the 
California Administrative Code; second, Clendenen possesses no 
authority with respect to any Agency decision beyond its pre-
sentation of recommendations concerning the water supply project 
(see subject contract, pp. 4-5); and third, Clendenen finances 
its own operations, operates as an independent contractor, and 
its research and conclusions in giving information, advice, 
recommendations and counsel are not subject to day-to-day review 
or direction by East Yolo (see subject contract, p. 23, ,X.C.). 
In a similar vein, Dr. Frank Clendenen, though he has 
no "corporate" status, has no personal contract with East Yolo, 
and operates solely as a corporate employee, and so, can claim 
the benefit of the second and third reasons why Clendenen isn't 
a "consultant" as specified above. 
For all the foregoing reasons, neither Clendenen nor 
Dr. Frank Clendenen is in a position to violate the spirit or 
the letter of the Political Reform Act, and we seek your advice 
confirming that fact. 
Assuming, solely for the sake of argument, that your 
advise were negative in this respect, Dr. Clendenen and 
Clendenen have one additional inquiry. 
In order to avoid any appearance of impropriety, and 
out of an abundance of caution, Clendenen has formally proposed 
to East Yolo that the subject contract be modified to exclude 
that portion of any system analysis and predesign work which would 
require an evaluation of systems encompassing those manufactured 
by Sierra Tech. 
That analysis and predesign work would be 
separately contracted for with another engineer of East Yolo's 
choosing, and the bill therefor would be charged by East Yolo 
solely to Clendenen. 
If such a proposal were accepted by East Yolo, and 
assuming solely for the sake of argument that Clendenen or Dr. 
Frank Clendenen would otherwise be in a potential conflict 
situation, would that modification of the subject contract remove 
any semblance of a possible violation of the Political Reform 
Act?

Ladies and Gentlemen: 
This firm represents Clendenen Engineers, Inc. a 
California Corporation ("Clendenen") and Dr. Frank Clendenen 
in connection with the interest which Clendenen now holds 
in a contract between Clendenen & Associates -
Consultants, Inc. 
and the East Yolo Community Services District ("East Yolo"). 
A 
copy of that contract ("the subject contract") is attached 
hereto for your reference as Exhibit "A". 
The subject contract,which was entered on March 6, 
1980, basically calls for system analysis and predesign work, 
final design and documentation, and construction administration 
and inspection by Clendenen for a domestic water supply project. 
Subsequent to entering the subject contract, Dr. 
Frank Clendenen, who is the sole stockholder of Clendenen, 
developed a patent for a modular filter system of a type which 
is sometimes used in water treatment plants. 
This system is 
manufactured and sold by Sierra Tech Systems, Inc., a California 
Corporation ("Sierra Tech") whose stock is owned one-hundred 
percent by Dr. Frank Clendenen. 
After development of the filter patent, a question 
was raised about whether Clendenen's role under the subject 
contract somehow conflicted with Dr. Frank Clendenen's role as 
owner of the stock in Sierra Tech Systems, Inc. 
The question 
was prompted by the fact that the Sierra Tech modular filter 


GREVE. CLIFFORD. DIEPENBROCK S PARAS

system was and is of a type that could be incorporated into the 
East Yolo water supply project. 
See subject contract, p. 3, 
§ 3 (a) • 
that: 
Section 87.100 of the Political Reform Act provides 
"No public official at any level of state 
or local government shall make, participate in 
making, or in any way attempt to use an official 
position to influence a governmental decision in 
which he knows or has reason to know he has a 
financial interest." 
In the common meaning of the word, neither Dr. Frank Clendenen 
nor Clendenen is a "public official" within the meaning of the 
Act. 
However, Government Code § 82048 states that: 
", [P}ublic official' means every member, 
officer, and employee or consultant of a state 
or local government agency." 
It is clear under California law that neither Dr. Frank 
Clendenen nor Clendenen is a "consultant" within the meaning of 
the Political Reform Act. 
California Administrative Code section 18700 defines 
a "consultant" as follows: 
", [C]onsultant' shall include any natural 
person who provides, under contract, information, 
advice, recommendation, or counsel to a state or 
local government agency, provided, however, that 
'consultant' shall not include a person who' (a) 
conducts research and arrives at conclusions with 
respect to his or her rendition of information, 
advice, recommendation, or counsel independent of 
the control and direction of the agency or of any 
agency official other than normal contract monitor-
ing; and (b) possesses no authority with respect to 
agency decision beyond the rendition of information, 
advice, recommendation, or counsel. ,II 
In light of this definition, Clendenen is not a 
consultant within the meaning of the Political Reform Act for 


GREVE, CLIFFORD. DIEPENBROCK S PARAS 
Office of Counsel 
Legal Division 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
December 12- 0984 
Page Three 
at least three reasons: 
First, Clendenen is a corporation, i.e .. 
a "legal" individual, but not a natural person as defined by the 
California Administrative Code; second, Clendenen possesses no 
authority with respect to any Agency decision beyond its pre-
sentation of recommendations concerning the water supply project 
(see subject contract, pp. 4-5); and third, Clendenen finances 
its own operations, operates as an independent contractor, and 
its research and conclusions in giving information, advice, 
recommendations and counsel are not subject to day-to-day review 
or direction by East Yolo (see subject contract, p. 23, 
~X.C.). 
In a similar vein, Dr. Frank Clendenen, though he has 
no "corporate" status, has no personal contract with East Yolo, 
and operates sOlely as a corporate employee, and so, can claim 
the benefit of the second and third reasons why Clendenen isn't 
a "consultant" as specified above. 
For all the foregoing reasons, neither Clendenen nor 
Dr. Frank Clendenen is in a position to violate the spirit or 
the letter of the Political Reform Act, and we seek your advice 
confirming that fact. 
Assuming, solely for the sake of argument, that your 
advise were negative in this respect, Dr. Clendenen and 
Clendenen have one additional inquiry. 
In order to avoid any appearance of impropriety, and 
out of an abundance of caution, Clendenen has formally proposed 
to East Yolo that the subject contract be modified to exclude 
that portion of any system analysis and predesign work which would 
require an evaluation of systems encompassing those manufactured 
by Sierra Tech. 
That analysis and predesign work would be 
separately contracted for with another engineer of East Yolo's 
choosing, and the bill therefor would be charged by East Yolo 
solely to Clendenen. 
If such a proposal were accepted by East Yolo, and 
assuming solely for the sake of argument that Clendenen or Dr. 
Frank Clendenen would otherwise be in a potential conflict 
situation, would that modification of the subject contract remove 
any semblance of a possible violation of the Political Reform 
Act?

-

Ladies and Gentlemen: 
This firm represents Clendenen Engineers, Inc. a 
California Corporation ("Clendenen") and Dr. Frank Clendenen 
in connection with the interest which Clendenen now holds 
in a contract between Clendenen & Associates -
Consultants, Inc. 
and the East Yolo Community Services District ("East Yolo"). 
A 
copy of that contract ("the subject contract") is attached 
hereto for your reference as Exhibit "A". 
The subject contract,which was entered on March 6, 
1980, basically calls for system analysis and predesign work, 
final design and documentation, and construction administration 
and inspection by Clendenen for a domestic water supply project. 
Subsequent to entering the subject contract, Dr. 
Frank Clendenen, who is the sole stockholder of Clendenen, 
developed a patent for a modular filter system of a type which 
is sometimes used in water treatment plants. 
This system is 
manufactured and sold by Sierra Tech Systems, Inc., a California 
Corporation ("Sierra Tech") whose stock is owned one-hundred 
percent by Dr. Frank Clendenen. 
After development of the filter patent, a question 
was raised about whether Clendenen's role under the subject 
contract somehow conflicted with Dr. Frank Clendenen's role as 
owner of the stock in Sierra Tech Systems, Inc. 
The question

Dean Stiles Ad",inistratiOtl 322.5660 Exacutive/l"9al •• Enfo,cat'IIent 322.5901 322-6441 January 17, 1985 Statements of Economic Interest 322~ Department of Veterans Affairs 1227 0 Street Sacramento, CA 95814 Dear Mr. Stiles: Re: Reporting of Reimbursements for Travel Our File No. A-84~307 Dave Matsler of the Department of Veterans Affairs has provided us with a copy of your letter regarding his reporting requirements on his Statement of Economic Interests as a result of his trip to New York in 1983 to make a presentation on behalf of the state to various bond rating agencies. In your letter, you stated that the entire cost of the trip was reimbursed by the underwriters who had been chosen to handle the Department's December 1983 revenue bond sale. You concluded that payments made by the underwriters for Mr. Matsler's transportation, lodging, and meals were gifts to the state which were not reportable by Mr. Matsler on his Statement of Economic Interests because Mr. Matsler was traveling on state business and could have received reimbursement from the state for those expenses. We have informed Mr. Matsler that the Commission's opinions and advice in similar situations do not support your conclusions. Section 82030(a) of the Government Codell states the general rule that "income" for purposes of the Political Reform Act includes any reimbursement for expenses. However, Section 82030(b) (2) specifies that reimbursement received from a state, local or federal government agency is excepted from the definition of income. 1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise noted. He Dean Stiles January 17, 1985 Page 2 The payments for Mr. Matsler's transportation, lodging, and meals were not made by a state, local, or federal government agency, but by the underwriters. Therefore, although Mr. Matsler was traveling on official state business, the transportation, lodging, and meals provided to Mr. Matsler are income within the meaning of Section 82030. More specifically, Mr. Matsler received a gift from the underwriters when he accepted transportation, food, and lodging at their expense. The fact that Mr. Matsler could have received reimbursement from the state for his transportation and meal expenses does not affect the above conclusion. The Commission has ruled that, under specific circumstances not here applicable, payment of a public official's transportation and other expenses by a private entity may be considered a gift to the state or local government agency rather than to the public official. In its opinion issued to Peter G. Stone, 3 FPPC Opinions 52 (No. 77-003, June 9, 1977), the Commission set forth the following four minimum criteria for determining when a gift is made to the state or local government agency rather than to the official: 1. The donor intended to donate the gift to the public agency and not to the official; 2. The public agency exercises substantial control over use of the gift;
